coredata.dc:title: The nanocomposite system comprising folic acid-modified graphene quantum dots loaded with evodiamine in the treatment of oral squamous cell carcinoma
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Yu
Liu, Yan
Wang, Yurui
Guo, Yanzhu
Li, Yuanyuan
Li, Ruizhi
Kong, Xinzi
Han, Qize
Wei, Rong
Wang, Jing
coredata.dc:description: Oral squamous cell carcinoma (OSCC) is one of the most common malignant tumors in the world. At present, drugs are one of the most indispensable means to treat OSCC. Evodiamine (EVO) is one of the major bioactive compounds of the plant Evodia and has been shown to inhibit the growth of OSCC cells. However, it suffers from poor aqueous solubility, which results in a low bioavailability. To improve of this property, we utilized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as a vehicle for the delivery of EVO, which were functionalized with folic acid (FA) for targeting the tumor cells. The GQDs-FA-EVO nanocomposite system was synthesized by a self-assembly method, which was simulated theoretically at the atomic scale using molecular dynamics calculations. These MD simulations confirmed that the binding of EVO to the FA-functionalized GQDS was spontaneous and resulted in the formation of a highly stable nanocomposite complex. When the loading of EVO into the composite system was 10%, the growth inhibition rate of OSCC cells exceeded 50%(p < 0.01). In tumor-bearing nude mice, the GQDs-FA-EVO composite loaded with 10% EVO significantly reduced the tumor volume by 19% compared to the EVO group after the 18-days treatment(p < 0.05).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Bioimaging
Evodiamine
Targeted drug delivery oral
Squamous cell carcinoma

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Geometrically optimized graphene quantum dots (GQDs), folic acid (FA), and Evodiamine (EVO) molecular structure models (Figure A), Figure B (a) is the initial state of MD simulation, (b) The figure is the final state of MD simulation.
Depicts the results of the carrier GQDs interacting with drug molecules under stable energy state.
TEM images of GQDs (A-B) and GQDs-FA-EVO (C). The hydrodynamic diameter of GQDs(D) and GQDs-FA-EVO(E) was measured by DLS. (F) Zeta potential of GQDs and GQDs-FA-EVO.

Optical properties of the GQDs (A), UV–vis absorption (red curve), photoluminescence excitation (green curve) and photoluminescence (pink curve) spectra of the aqueous GQDs solution. UV–vis absorption spectra of GQDs-FA (B) and GQDs-FA-EVO (C). (D) FTIR spectra of FA, GQDs-FA, EVO and GQDs-FA-EVO. Fluorescence spectra of GQDs(E) and GQDs-FA-EVO(F) under different excitation wavelengths. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Confocal laser fluorescence images of tumor cells labeled with GQDs (B) and GQDs-FA-EVO (E) (A and D dark field; B and E bright field; C is the composite image of A and B; the image F is the composite image of D and E.) Scale bar: 20 µm.

(A) CCK-8 experiment to determine the toxicity of different concentrations of GQDs, GQDs-FA, EVO and GQDs-FA-EVO to tumor cells (mean ± SD, n = 5, *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001), (B) The cell clone formation ability of the medium (a), GQDs (b), GQDs-FA (c), EVO (d), and GQDs-FA-EVO (e) groups. (C) Medium group, GQDs, GQDs-FA, EVO and GQDs-FA-EVO cell apoptosis results. (D) Fluorescence microscope to observe the relative activity of Calcein-AM/PI co-staining medium, GQDs, GQDs-FA, EVO and GQDs-FA-EVO treated cells. Green and red represent live and dead cells, respectively. Scale bar: 200 µm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Pictures of representative mice obtained in each group after treatment. (B) A representative picture of the tumor peeled off after treatment. (C) Average tumor weight curve. (D) Changes in tumor volume (mean ± SD, n = 5, *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001). (E) Changes in body weight during treatment. (F) Physiological saline was injected into the tail vein. (G) Representative fluorescence photos of GQDs-FA-EVO 24 h after tail vein injection.
H&E staining results of tumor sections and vital organs. Scale bar: 100 µm.

head.ce:title: The nanocomposite system comprising folic acid-modified graphene quantum dots loaded with evodiamine in the treatment of oral squamous cell carcinoma

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School/Hospital of Stomatology, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School/Hospital of Stomatology, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Clinical Research Center of Shanxi Province for Dental and Maxillofacial Diseases College of Stomatology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School/Hospital of Stomatology, Lanzhou University Lanzhou 730000, PR China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed to the work equally and should be regarded as co-first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: GQDs-FA-EVO self-assembly synthesis process, targeting and drug treatment, and tumor labeling.

Oral squamous cell carcinoma (OSCC) is one of the most common malignant tumors in the world. At present, drugs are one of the most indispensable means to treat OSCC. Evodiamine (EVO) is one of the major bioactive compounds of the plant Evodia and has been shown to inhibit the growth of OSCC cells. However, it suffers from poor aqueous solubility, which results in a low bioavailability. To improve of this property, we utilized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as a vehicle for the delivery of EVO, which were functionalized with folic acid (FA) for targeting the tumor cells. The GQDs-FA-EVO nanocomposite system was synthesized by a self-assembly method, which was simulated theoretically at the atomic scale using molecular dynamics calculations. These MD simulations confirmed that the binding of EVO to the FA-functionalized GQDS was spontaneous and resulted in the formation of a highly stable nanocomposite complex. When the loading of EVO into the composite system was 10%, the growth inhibition rate of OSCC cells exceeded 50%(p < 0.01). In tumor-bearing nude mice, the GQDs-FA-EVO composite loaded with 10% EVO significantly reduced the tumor volume by 19% compared to the EVO group after the 18-days treatment(p < 0.05).

ce:section.ce:para: Oral squamous cell carcinoma (OSCC) is the predominant (∼95% occurrence) histological type of oral cancer [1,2]. With the gradual increase in the rate of OSCC morbidity and mortality, the overall five-year survival rate is<60% [3,4]. Even if patients receive standard surgical and non-surgical treatments, the recurrence rate of OSCC is still 18–76% [5]. In recent years, the rise in early tumor screening and development of more-efficacious treatment methods has led to a significant increase in the survival rate of patients with OSCC compared to the past few decades; on the contrary, the related mortality rate has not decreased significantly [5]. At present, surgical intervention is the most common method for treating OSCC, and chemotherapy and radiotherapy are often supplemented to prevent recurrence after surgical removal of the tumor [6]. However, traditional treatment methods have limitations and serious side effects, including serious trauma, low efficacy, and normal tissue damage [7]. Therefore, drug intervention is a necessary means to improve the survival rate of patients with OSCC. Evodiamine (EVO) is a plant-derived indolequinazoline alkaloid, which is one of the major bioactive compounds of the plant. Our previous research found that EVO can effectively inhibit the proliferation of OSCC cells [8,9]. In recent years, EVO has been widely reported as a potential therapeutic drug for breast, lung, colon, and liver cancer [10–13]. However, EVO is poorly water-soluble and selective for cancer cells, and it cannot be delivered to tumor tissues in its free form because of its low bioavailability, which largely limits its clinical application [14]. Therefore, improving the water solubility of EVO and its bioavailability is a major challenge that has piqued our interest.

To overcome these limitations, it was proposed that nano-drug delivery systems could increase the water solubility of EVO and improve its bioavailability to improve the delivery efficiency of the drug to cancer cells. In recent years, multifunctional nanocomposite drugs with varying physical and chemical properties have offered new hope for cancer treatment [15–17]. Nanocomposite drug delivery systems have improved the water solubility, dose resistance, safety, and therapeutic efficacy of drugs [18–20]. Currently, owing to the impressive progress in materials science and pharmacy, a wide range of organic or nanocarriers with different sizes, structures and surface properties have been designed. The inorganic-based nanomaterials include magnetic nanoparticles [21], gold nanoparticles [22], and carbon nanomaterials [23], while the organic-based materials include liposomes and polymeric micelles[24,25]. However, many of these drug-delivery vehicles are cytotoxic, which limits their applicability. Among these nanocarrier candidates, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have received significant attention recently due to their excellent versatility. GQDs are a zero-dimensional carbon material derived from graphene. Due to quantum confinement, edge effects, and surface structure, GQDs exhibit excellent physical, chemical, and biological properties. For example, the tunable optical properties and excellent photostability of GQDs make them suitable candidates for optical imaging applications [26–28]. Furthermore, GQDs have a large specific surface area and can be used as potential candidates for drug delivery platforms. Their delocalized π-bonds can form specific π-π interactions with other aromatic hydrocarbons, offering unique advantages to enhance the aqueous solubility of poorly water-soluble, aromatic ring-containing drugs [29,30].

Previously, Song et al. synthesized a nanocomposite system based on GQDs that were efficiently loaded with the hydrophobic anticancer drug paclitaxel (DTX) for lung cancer treatment [20]. Yang et al. synthesized a GQD loaded with the anticancer drug adriamycin (DOX) for the treatment of different malignancies [29,31]. Shi et al. effectively improved the efficacy of the anticancer drug cisplatin (IV) by loading it onto GQDs [32]. GQDs were found to not only be suitable carriers for drugs but also overcome the physiological barriers of drugs to tumors by enhancing drug permeability and retention (i.e. Enhanced Permeability and Retention (EPR) effect) in tumor tissues [33,34]. The EPR effect refers to the ability of non-targeted nanomedicines to accumulate in tumor tissues; thus, nanomedicines with higher EPR effects will demonstrate increased efficacy and lower systemic toxicities. However, GQDs do not have the ability to specifically recognize tumors, so tumor targeting is poor. Instead, exploiting the use of targeting molecules can enable the selective binding of the GQDs to antigens or receptors on the surface of tumor cells, thereby enhancing the retention of nanomedicines at the tumor site and exerting therapeutic effects more effectively [18,35,36]. Interestingly, GQDs have a large number of reactive functional groups on their surface that can serve as loading sites for targeted small molecules, such as folic acid (FA) [18]. Because the folate receptor (FR) is expressed in significantly higher proportions in many different tumor tissues compared to normal tissues, GQDs can be functionalized with FA ligands, which bind to FRs overexpressed on the surface of cancer cells to assist the GQDS in selectively targeting tumor cells over normal cells [37–40].

Inspired by previous research that aimed to refine the current traditional treatment methods for OSCC patients, we sought to developed a multifunctional platform for the targeted nano-delivery of anticancer drugs to OSCC cells. In this study, FA-decorated GQDs were used as drug EVO carriers to synthesize GQDs-FA-EVO nanocomposite system by layer-by-layer self-assembly method. The GQDs-FA-EVO nanocomposite system to improve the aqueous solubility and selectivity of EVO toward OSCC cells, with the goal to not only develop a platform to selectively recognize and kill OSCC cells through folic acid, but also reduce the toxicity and off-target side effects of anticancer drugs, including EVO. With the continuous development of nanomedicines, imaging-guided cancer treatments with multifunctional drug delivery systems offer greater possibilities for precision therapy compared to conventional treatment options [41,42]. Therefore, it is necessary to develop intelligent nano-drug delivery systems that enable safe, effective, and personalized anti-tumor treatments, including for the treatment of OSCC.

Folic acid was obtained from Sigma., Ltd., U.S. Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) was obtained from Shanghai Yi sheng Biological Technology Co., Ltd., China. Annexin V-FITC/PI apoptosis detection kit was purchased from Dalian Meilun Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Jimsa was purchased from Soleibao Technology Co., Ltd. Calcein-AM/PI Cell Viability was purchased from Beyotian Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Dimethyl sulfoxide was purchased from sigma Co., Ltd. U.S. All reagents were used directly without further purification.

The instruments used in experiments included Tecnai F30 transmission electron microscope (FEI, The Netherlands), Dynamic light scattering (DLS) (90Plus Pals) (Brookhaven, USA), UV–vis spectrophotometer (Unicor Instrument Co., Ltd., China), Fluorescence Spectrometer (FL3-2,Horiba, Japan), Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (NEXUS670, NICOLET, USA), Fluorescence microscope (BX53, Olympus, Japan), Confocal laser scanning microscope (FV3000, Olympus, Japan), Light microscope (BX53, Olympus, Japan) VISQUE Invivo Smart-LE (Viework, China). flow cytometer (BD Inc, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation program of GQDs-FA-EVO nanocomposite system
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, the Materials Studio (MS) software is used to build the system model, optimize the surface adsorption structure of the system, and perform molecular dynamics simulation and the final adsorption energy calculation [43,44].

The size of the Graphene quantum dots is about 50 × 50 Å^2, and the dangling bonds at the edges are saturated with hydrogen atoms. The forcefield parameters of the carrier GQDs and the drug component EVO are taken from the COMPASS Field. When using periodic boundary conditions, all simulation boxes are selected sufficiently large to prevent the cells in the simulation system from interacting with the cells in adjacent cells. In all simulation systems, the drug component is located approximately 2 nm from the surface of the carrier.

Current operation, the initial structures are geometrically optimized through the CASTEP module, and the molecular dynamics (MD) simulation is performed through the use of the Forcite + module in MS. The ensemble selects NVT, and the temperature is maintained at 298.0 K. All production MDs are executed under 60 ns periodic boundary conditions for each system [44].

Eads (adsorption energy) calculation formula:
E
ads
=
E
GQDs - F A - E V O
-
(
E
GQDS - F A
+
E
EVO
)

Among them, EGQDs-FA-EVO, EGQDs-FA and EEVO represent the energy of the entire composite system of GQDs-FA-EVO, GQDs-FA and EVO molecules, respectively. The larger the negative value, the stronger the adsorption and the more stable the system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of GQDs, GQDs-FA and GQDs-FA-EVO
ce:section.ce:para: Here, GQDs were prepared using an electrochemical workstation with a constant current of 0.01A. at room temperature (25 °C). Firstly, round graphite rods are used as anode and cathode electrodes, and the two electrodes are separated by 2 cm. Typically, 300 mL of 0.2 mol/L ammonia solution served as the electrolyte to cut the graphene paper and act as amino source. The resulting solution was centrifuged by 220 nm syringe filters with membranes to remove the large non-luminescent fraction. After filtering, dialysis bag with the molecular weight of 3500 Da was used to remove the unreacted reagents and ammonia molecule. Ultrapure water was replaced at least 10 times. 50 mg of FA were dissolved in 5 mL DMSO at room temperature. Then, 25 mg of synthesized GQDs was added to the reaction mixture and stirred for 18 h. Finally, the GQDs-FA mixture was filtered to remove the unreacted substances and stored at 4 °C. 250 µg EVO were dissolved in 25 mg GQDs-FA and ice bath ultrasound for 10 min while avoiding the light. After similar purification steps as explained above, GQDs-FA-EVO was obtained and stored in dark.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro laser scanning confocal image
ce:section.ce:para: The Cal-27 cells were seeded into confocal capsule at 2.5 × 105/well and treated with GQDs or GQDs-FA-EVO for 4 h. Removing the culture medium, PBS was used to wash the cells thrice to get rid of free probe. The cells were immobilized with 4% Paraformaldehyde and after which, they were observed using laser scanning confocal microscopy.

The Cal-27 cells were seeded in 96-well plate at 5 × 103/ well and treated with GQDs, GQDs-FA, EVO and GQDs-FA-EVO with different concentrations. After incubation for 48 h, 20 μLcck-8 solution was injected each well and maintained for 2 h. Then the absorbance was measured at 450 nm to calculate the cell survival rate.

The Cal-27cells were seeded in 6-well plate at 1.5 × 103/ well and treated with GQDs, GQDs-FA, EVO and GQDs-FA-EVO for 48 h, then removing the culture medium, PBS was used to wash the cells thrice and treated with fresh medium After culturing for 7 days, colonies were fixed and stained with Methanol and Jimsa. The number of colonies was then determined using ImageJ.

The Cal-27 cells were seeded in 6-well plate at 2.5 × 105/ well and treated with GQDs, GQD-FA, EVO and GQDs-FA-EVO for 48 h, then removing the culture medium, PBS was used to wash the cells thrice to get rid of free probe. Calcein-AM and PI were used to stain the cells for 30 min at 37℃ in the dark, after which, they were observed using inverted optical fluorescence microscope.

The Cal-27 cells were seeded in 6-well plate at 5 × 105/ well and treated with GQDs, GQD-FA, EVO and GQDs-FA-EVO for 48 h. Then removing the culture medium, all of the cells were washed twice with PBS and harvested by trypsinization, followed by centrifugation at 1500 rpm for 5 min. The precipitate obtained was re-suspended in PBS.5 μL Annexin V-FITC and 10 μL PI were used to stain the cells for 10 min at room temperature in the dark and analyzed using a flow cytometer.

When the tumor size reached around 100 mm3, Saline, GQDs-FA-EVO (200 μL, 20 mg/kg) were administered to the BALB/c mice bearing Cal-27 tumors via tail injection. Next, in vivo whole-body fluorescent imaging was performed with an imaging system at the 480 nm excitation wavelength after 24 h post-injection.

When the tumor volume reached ≥ 100 mm3, Cal-27 tumor-bearing mice were randomly divided into 5 groups (5 mice/group), and 200 μL of different solutions were tail vein injection: Saline, GQDs, GQDs-FA, EVO and GQDs-FA-EVO. Daily recording of the body weights, and tumor volumes was performed throughout the treatment. To calculated the tumor volume, the following formula was applied:
V
= ( l e n g t h × w i d t
h 2
) / 2 .
After the end of the 18-days treatment, the tumor tissues were carried out after sacrificing mice for further histochemical analysis using H&E.

The major organs including heart, liver, spleen, lung and kidney were collected simultaneously. After they were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde, dehydrated, sliced and stained with H&E, the histopathologic examination was performed using light microscope with a magnification of 10 × 40.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Molecular dynamics (MD) simulations of the GQDs-FA-EVO ternary composite system
ce:section.ce:para:
The potential of GQDs carriers as EVO drug delivery systems was investigated using molecular dynamics simulations. For this purpose, four simulation systems, GQDs, FA, GQDs-FA, EVO and GQDs-FA-EVO are constructed. Details of all of systems are listed in Fig. 1 . Fig. 1A shows the optimized geometric structure model of GQDs, FA, and EVO, while Fig. 1B depicts the structural model before and after molecular dynamics simulation between EVO, FA, and the GQDs carrier. Fig. 1A is the initial energy state of the GQDs-FA-EVO ternary composite system, and the final energy state is shown in Fig. 1B. In the MD simulations, when folic acid molecules and drug molecules were located on both sides of the GQDs carrier the energy released by the adsorption of EVO to the GQDs-FA ternary composite system (Eads) was −109.0525 kcal/mol, indicating that the adsorption of the drug molecule (EVO) on the surface of the carrier (GQDs) is spontaneous and results in the formation of a stable complex (Fig. 2 ). The Eads was calculated according the following equation below:
E
ads
=
E
GQDs - F A - E V O
-
(
E
GQDs - F A
+
E
EVO
)

in which the total energy of the GQDs-FA-EVO nanocomposite system (EGQDs-FA-EVO) was 12291.8461 kcal/mol, while the molecular energy of the GQDs-FA (EGQDs-FA) was 12342.0228 kcal/mol, and the molecular energy of EVO (EEVO) was 58.8758 kcal/mol. When both folic acid molecules and drug molecules are located on one side of GQDs, GQDs-FA-EVO nanocomposite system was −63.9305 kcal/mol, the absolute value of the total energy of the composite system was lower than that of the folic acid molecules and drug molecules were located on both sides of the GQDs. Therefore, when folic acid and EVO molecules were adsorbed onto both sides of the GQDs, the structure of the ternary composite system was more stable than the neat GQDs. In addition, the distances of the interactions observed between the EVO molecules and the GQDs carrier predominantly ranged between 3.0 and 4.0 Å, which were very close to the distances of π-π interactions in similar systems, indicating that EVO molecules could form strong π-π stacking interactions with the aromatic rings on the surface of the GQDs. Therefore, the results of the MD simulations provided sufficient theoretical support for subsequent experiments and facilitated the development and design of other ternary nanocomposite drug delivery systems.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was employed to the study the morphology and structure of the GQDs and GQDs-FA-EVO. As shown in the TEM images of GQDs (Fig. 3 A and 3B) and GQDs-FA-EVO (Fig. 3C). The GQDs exhibited uniform distribution, and with an average diameter of 3 nm. The GQDs-FA-EVO composite particles had a relatively smooth surface, and no large agglomerations. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis further demonstrated that the sizes of the GQDs and GQDs-FA-EVO nanoparticles were uniform (Fig. 3D and 3E). The sizes of the GQDs nanoparticles were mainly in the range of 5–8 nm, while the GQDs-FA-EVO nanoparticles were mainly in the range of 85–130 nm. The uniform size nanoparticles promote and enhance their permeability and retention (EPR) effect at the tumor site, so the uniform size GQDs-FA-EVO ternary complex system further increases passive targeting and accumulation around the tumor for therapeutic effect. After the coupling FA and loading EVO to the surface of the GQDs particles, the surface average zeta potential of the resulting GQDs-FA-EVO composite increased from − 32.9 mV to − 16.6 mV (Fig. 3F), indicating that the ternary composite system featured a weaker negative charge after incorporation of the small molecules into the carrier. The more positively charged nanoparticles will non-specifically bind to the negatively charged components on the surface of blood vessel walls through electrostatic interactions and will be quickly cleared through the blood circulation, thereby reducing their propensity to spread deeper in tumors and preventing their distribution in the systemic circulation. While the more negatively charged particles will be taken up by the liver, resulting in slower excretion from the body and possible toxicity. At the same time, highly negatively charged nanoparticles can cause blood flocculation, the clinical consequences of which are very serious. To ensure a suitable EPR effect and maximize the efficacy of drugs in vivo, nanoparticle-based drug-delivery systems need to be biocompatible, have a suitable size, and either a weakly negative zeta potential or be neutral.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the aqueous GQDs solution featured an absorption peak at 228 nm corresponding to the typical π-π* transition of the sp2 domain of the GQDs as well as an absorption peak at 270 nm corresponding to the n-π* transition. The green and pink curves represent the optimal excitation wavelength (λ ex) and the corresponding emission wavelength (λ em) at 353 nm and 460 nm, respectively (Fig. 1A). Fig. 4 B shows representative absorption spectra of the GQDs, FA, and GQDs-FA. The FA moieties were decorated to the af-GQDs surfaces by a crosslinking reaction between carboxylic acid groups of FA and amine group of N-GQDs. The absorption spectrum of the GQD-FA featured typical absorption peaks at 280 nm and 360 nm, indicating the GQDs were successfully functionalized with FA. After loading EVO onto the surface of the GQDs-FA, the absorption spectrum of GQDs-FA-EVO featured a typical characteristic absorption peak at the corresponding wavelength of 250 mm. Compared to the absorption maximum of EVO alone, the absorption maximum corresponding to EVO in the UV–vis spectrum of GQD-FA-EVO was red-shifted by 52 nm, indicating that the drug had been successfully loaded onto the GQDs (Fig. 4C). This red shift was typically attributed to the π-π superposition and hydrophobic interactions between EVO and GQDs. The FTIR spectra of the FA, and GQDs-FA are presented in Fig. 4D. The absorption of FA at 3415 cm−1 is due to hydroxy (OH) stretching vibration. Absorption peaks at 3546 cm−1 and 3322 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of the aromatic amine. When the GQDs were functionalized with FA, new absorption peaks emerged at 1693 cm−1 and 1606 cm−1 and were attributed to the stretching vibrations of the C = O and N–H bonds, respectively. These characteristic absorption peaks corroborated the formation of amide bonds between the GQDs and FA. The absorption peaks of EVO at 1629 cm−1 and 1510 cm−1 predominantly corresponded to the stretching vibrations of the carbonyl (C = O) group and the C = C bonds of the benzene ring in EVO. The FTIR spectrum of the GQDs-FA-EVO featured a peak characteristic of EVO at 1510 cm−1, indicating that EVO was successfully loaded onto the GQDs-FA. The photoluminescence spectra of GQDs(D) and GQDs-FA-EVO(E) under excitation at different wavelengths. The fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs indicates diversified surface states such as the diversified oxygen-containing groups. The PL spectra of GQDs-FA-EVO in aqueous solutions demonstrated an obvious excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence behavior under different excitation wavelengths. Therefore, these unique PL characteristics and good biocompatibility endowed the ternary composite system with the potential for applications in tumor cell bioimaging.

Fig. 5 A–C show the confocal laser microscopy images of the GQDs in Cal-27 cells. GQDs was found to localize to the cell after internalization. The GQDs-FA-EVO were also effectively taken up into the cells, as evidenced by the bright green fluorescence within the cells (Fig. 5D–F).

CCK-8 assays were run to assess the effects of different concentrations of GQDs, GQDs-FA, EVO, and GQDs-FA-EVO on the proliferation of Cal-27 cells. After incubation of the Cal-27 cells with the GQDs and GQDs-FA (controls), cell viabilities greater than 90% were calculated, while the GQDs-FA-EVO (experimental group) significantly inhibited the growth of tumor cells and Cal-27 cells in a dose-dependent manner. However, the proliferation inhibition of the Cal-27 cells by GQDs-FA-EVO was not as significant compared to EVO alone. This result might have been the result of FA significantly reducing the cytotoxicity of EVO and was consistent with the MD simulations results. Clone formation experiments were performed to determine the influence of both the control group and the experimental group on the proliferation of tumor cells. Fig. 6 B shows the images of cell colony formation in medium (a), GQDs (b), GQDs-FA (c), EVO (d), and GQDs-FA-EVO (e) groups, respectively. The colony formation ability of Cal-27 cells in the culture medium, GQDs, and GQDs-FA treatment groups is greater than 70%, and the colony formation ability of cells in the GQDs-FA-EVO group is about 50%, so GQDS-FA-EVO can inhibit the colony formation ability of Cal-27 cells. In addition to cell proliferation, the ability of the GQDs, GQDs-FA, EVO and GQDs-FA-EVO to induce apoptosis of the Cal-27 cells was assessed using the Annexin V-FITC/PI double staining method (Fig. 6C). Compared to the three control groups, the GQDs-FA-EVO nanoparticles significantly induced apoptosis in the cancer cells. Fig. 6D presents the fluorescence microscopy images of the Cal-27 cells treated with GQDs, GQDs-FA, EVO and GQDs-FA-EVO after Cal-27 cells were incubated with the materials and Calcein-AM/PI dye. The green and red colors indicated live and dead cells, respectively. We found that the images of the GQDs-FA-EVO experimental group featured more red fluorescence, which indicated that the ternary composite system was effective in reducing tumor cell viability, while the control groups had negligible effects on cell viability.

Representative mice were selected and administered the different treatment groups, after which pictures were taken, and the treatment results were recorded (Fig. 7 A). During the treatment period, the tumor volume and the weight of the mice in different groups were monitored every two days. After the treatment, the mice were sacrificed, the tumor tissue was dissected, and the volume and average tumor weight of each group were measured. As shown in the photos, the GQDs-FA-EVO experimental group showed the best treatment effect. Treatment of the mice with the GQDs-FA-EVO resulted in the tumors with the smallest volume (Fig. 7B). The average tumor tissue weight of the GQDs-FA-EVO-treated mice was also significantly lower than the others groups (Fig. 7B), corroborating the previously demonstrated anti-proliferation efficacy in vitro (Cal-27 cells). After 18 days of treatment, it was found that the tumor volume in the GQDs-FA-EVO group increased the slowest (Fig. 7D), and there were no significant differences in mouse body weight between each group (Fig. 7E), suggesting that our synthesized materials were biologically safe and did not provide any toxic side effects. To determine the tissue distribution of the nanomaterials throughout the mice, Cal-27 tumor-bearing nude mice were administered intravenously (iv) 200 µL of a 10 mg/kg solution of the GQDs-FA-EVO in × through the caudal vein (tail), and its distribution throughout the body was measured by in vivo fluorescence imaging using an IVIS Spectrum Imaging System after 24 h (Fig. 7G). The fluorescence was highly localized at the tumor site, which indicated that the GQDs-FA-EVO was able to selectively target the tumor and be retained at the tumor site.

Fig. 8 shows the pathology results of tumor tissues and important organs of heart, liver, spleen, lung and kidney the different treatment groups. The tumor cells in the Saline, GQDs and GQDs-FA groups had different shapes, with obvious atypia, poor differentiation and irregular shape. Both the nuclei of EVO and GQDs-FA-EVO treatment groups became smaller, indicating apoptosis/necrosis. To further evaluate the potential systemic toxicity of the prepared nanomaterials, the mice in the different treatment groups were sacrificed after conclusion of the experiments, and the main organs (heart, liver, spleen, kidney and lung) were dissected for histological analysis. After staining of the cells that comprised the organ tissues dissected from the mice with H&E stain, a clear nuclei structure, no signs of necrosis, and no morphological abnormalities or obvious pathological changes were observed in all treatment groups. These results indicated that the nanomaterials demonstrated almost no toxic side effects in the organs, further highlighting their biological safety.

In this study, GQDs were functionalized with FA to develop a carrier system for the targeted delivery of the anti-cancer drug EVO to overcome the poor water solubility of EVO. MD simulations calculations aimed at elucidating the interactions between EVO and the GQDs that enabled drug loading indicated that strong π-π interactions facilitated the spontaneous adsorption of EVO onto the surface of the GQDs particles under certain conditions to form a stable complex. Compared to the other complex methods of synthesizing drug delivery systems, the GQDs-FA-EVO composite system was successfully synthesized by undergoing self-assembly using the chemisorption energy in the system, resulting in size uniformity within the system. Both in vitro and in vivo experimental results demonstrated that the GQDs-FA-EVO nanocomposite was highly biocompatible and exhibited low toxicity in cells and mice. Furthermore, the EVO drug molecules were efficiently loaded onto the nanoparticles, and the nanocomposite could selectively target tumor cells to deliver the drug to the cells to exert its anti-tumor properties, highlighting the possibilities of this system and others alike in the synergistic targeted therapy of OSCC.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China. Evodiamine inhibits the metastasis of tongue squamous cell carcinoma by regulating the ZMIZ1 gene and affecting the Notch signaling pathway, National Natural Science Foundation of China, Grant/Award Number:81773942. The project was funded by the Scientific and Technology Foundation of Gansu province (20JR10FA670), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities(lzujbky-2020-52), Lanzhou Talent Innovation and Entrepreneurship Project (2020-RC-103), Clinical Research Center of Shanxi Province for Dental and Maxillofacial Diseases, College of Stomatology, Xi’an Jiaotong University (2021YHJB05) and ZMIZ1 affects Wilms tumor metastasis through Notch signaling pathway. Gansu Provincial Department of Science and Technology (21ZR1RA13).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matdes.2022.110838.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
